examples.coaching: I'd play Nxc6. Your castling move is understandable, but it misses the more urgent problem: Black’s c6-knight is already attacking your c2-bishop. Play Nxc6 instead. That removes Black’s dangerous central pawn, wins the bishop, and your king still gets checked.
examples.takeaway: When your king is attacked, look for a move that removes the attacker’s central pawn or removes your attacked piece.

examples.coaching: I'd play Nxg6. That is a very natural follow-up because your g6-knight is attacked, so you need to answer it. The problem is that after Nxg6, Black can recapture on g6, so your central pawn is gone. Play b4 instead: it defends g6, keeps your g6-knight, and also keeps your c6-bishop. examples.takeaway: When your attacked piece is also attacked, answer it before recapturing it.
